channel.title: Nature Materials
channel.description: Nature Materials is a multidisciplinary journal aimed at bringing together cutting-edge research across the entire spectrum of materials science and technology. Nature Materials covers all applied and fundamental aspects of the synthesis/processing, structure/composition, properties and performance of materials. Nature Materials provides a forum for the development of a common identity among materials scientists while encouraging researchers to cross established subdisciplinary lines. To achieve this, Nature Materials takes an interdisciplinary, integrated and balanced approach to all areas of materials research while fostering the exchange of ideas between scientists involved in different communities.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: Revealing key structures for reversible sulfur redox in amorphous polymeric sulfur
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 28 January 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02484-y

Amorphous sulfurized polyacrylonitrile suppresses polysulfide shuttling in Li–S batteries, enabling long cycle life, but the structural processes involved during synthesis and initial cycling remained unclear. An operando pair distribution function, wide-angle scattering and sulfur absorption spectroscopy reveal S–C bond formation, π–π stacking and sulfur-chain shortening, which enable reversible sulfur redox.
item.dc:title: Revealing key structures for reversible sulfur redox in amorphous polymeric sulfur
item.dc:creator: Nan Wang
Shen Wang
Yu Zheng
Dacheng Kuai
Seungmin Lee
Sha Tan
Dean Yen
Hui Zhong
Sanjit Ghose
Yonghua Du
Perla Balbuena
Ping Liu
Enyuan Hu

item.title: Exploiting and taming the structural instability for computer memories
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 27 January 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02481-1

A phase-change material enables in-memory computing by well-controlled multilevel switching without resistance drift.
item.dc:title: Exploiting and taming the structural instability for computer memories
item.dc:creator: A. Lindsay Greer

item.title: Intertwined orders in a quantum-entangled metal
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 27 January 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-025-02475-5

Resonant inelastic X-ray scattering interferometry reveals a highly entangled electronic phase in Nd2Ir2O7, enabling extraction of its entanglement structure and confirming the cubic-symmetry-breaking order predicted from complementary Raman spectroscopy.
item.dc:title: Intertwined orders in a quantum-entangled metal
item.dc:creator: Junyoung Kwon
Jaehwon Kim

B. J. Kim

item.title: Narrowband quantum emitters in hexagonal boron nitride with optically addressable spins
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 27 January 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-025-02458-6

Producing isolated single-photon emitters in hexagonal boron nitride with predefined spin transitions is challenging. Oxygen annealing enables the controlled fabrication of narrowband quantum emitters with optically active spin for quantum applications.
item.dc:title: Narrowband quantum emitters in hexagonal boron nitride with optically addressable spins
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Whitefield
Helen Zhi Jie Zeng
James Liddle-Wesolowski
Islay O. Robertson
Ádám Ganyecz
Viktor Ivády
Kenji Watanabe
Takashi Taniguchi
Milos Toth
Jean-Philippe Tetienne
Igor Aharonovich
Mehran Kianinia

item.title: The paradoxes of interfacial electrolyte structures
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 26 January 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-025-02468-4

Charge frustration emerges as a key factor to explain the formation of overcharged ion structures next to an electrified surface.
item.dc:title: The paradoxes of interfacial electrolyte structures
item.dc:creator: Ricardo Garcia

item.title: Achieving high tensile strength and ductility in refractory alloys by tuning electronic structure
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 26 January 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-025-02464-8

Guided by density functional theory, a commercially viable refractory alloy is developed, which exhibits excellent room-temperature ductility and strength, and high strength at elevated temperatures.
item.dc:title: Achieving high tensile strength and ductility in refractory alloys by tuning electronic structure
item.dc:creator: Hailong Huang
Prashant Singh
Duane D. Johnson

item.title: How charge frustration causes ion ordering and microphase separation at surfaces
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 26 January 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-025-02467-5

Atomic force microscopy is used to investigate the adsorption and organization of ions on charged surfaces. Trivalent ions adopt complex networks, clusters and layers associated with overcharging, whereas divalent ions follow classical predictions.
item.dc:title: How charge frustration causes ion ordering and microphase separation at surfaces
item.dc:creator: Mingyi Zhang
Benjamin A. Legg
Benjamin A. Helfrecht
Yuanzhong Zhang
Shuai Tan
